section.title: The Glory Departs the Temple
section.sectionRationale: Ezekiel's inaugural throne vision by the River Kebar reappears inside the temple itself, but only to leave it. Having exposed the idolatry hidden within the house that bears His Name, God's glory rises from the Most Holy Place, pauses at the threshold, and moves at last to the east gate, a slow, visible withdrawal that proves no building, however sacred, can hold a God whose people no longer want Him.
section.thesis: Because Jerusalem's temple has been filled with idols, the glory of the LORD deliberately withdraws stage by stage, from above the cherubim to the threshold to the east gate, showing that God's presence was never a fixture of the building but a Person who can, and does, leave; yet this same glory is promised to return, a hope realized when the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the man in linen sent among the cherubim to scatter burning coals of judgment over the city; a detailed vision of the cherubim and their wheels, the same living creatures Ezekiel saw by the Kebar, now named; and the glory's unhurried departure, first above the cherubim, then to the threshold, then out to the east gate of the LORD's house.

units.label: Coals for the city
units.note: judgment scattered from among the cherubim

verses.text: And I looked and saw above the expanse, above the heads of the cherubim, the likeness of a throne of sapphire.

And the LORD said to the man clothed in linen, “Go inside the {{galgal|wheelwork}} beneath the cherubim. Fill your hands with burning coals from among the cherubim and scatter them over the city.” And as I watched, he went in.
Now when the man went in, the cherubim were standing on the south side of the temple, and a cloud filled the inner court.

Then the {{kavod|glory}} of the LORD rose from above the cherubim and stood over the threshold of the temple. The temple was filled with the cloud, and the court was filled with the brightness of the glory of the LORD.
The sound of the wings of the cherubim could be heard as far as the outer court, like the voice of God Almighty when He speaks.
When the LORD commanded the man clothed in linen, saying, “Take fire from within the wheelwork, from among the cherubim,” the man went in and stood beside a wheel.
Then one of the cherubim reached out his hand and took some of the fire that was among them. And he put it into the hands of the man clothed in linen, who received it and went out.

units.label: The cherubim and their wheels
units.note: the living creatures from the Kebar, named at last

verses.text: (The cherubim appeared to have the form of human hands under their wings.)
Then I looked and saw four wheels beside the cherubim, one wheel beside each {{keruv|cherub}}. And the wheels gleamed like a beryl stone.
As for their appearance, all four had the same form, like a wheel within a wheel.
When they moved, they would go in any of the four directions, without turning as they moved. For wherever the head faced, the cherubim would go in that direction, without turning as they moved.
Their entire bodies, including their backs, hands, and wings, were full of eyes all around, as were their four wheels.
I heard the wheels being called “the whirling {{galgal|wheels}}.”
Each of the cherubim had four faces: the first face was that of a cherub, the second that of a man, the third that of a lion, and the fourth that of an eagle.
Then the cherubim rose upward. These were the living creatures I had seen by the River Kebar.
When the cherubim moved, the wheels moved beside them, and even when they spread their wings to rise from the ground, the wheels did not veer away from their side.
When the cherubim stood still, the wheels also stood still, and when they ascended, the wheels ascended with them, for the spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels.

units.label: The glory departs
units.note: from the Most Holy Place to the threshold to the east gate

verses.text: Then the glory of the LORD moved away from the threshold of the temple and stood above the cherubim.

As I watched, the cherubim lifted their wings and rose up from the ground, with the wheels beside them as they went. And they stopped at the entrance of the east gate of the house of the LORD, with the glory of the God of Israel above them.
These were the living creatures I had seen beneath the God of Israel by the River Kebar, and I knew that they were cherubim.
Each had four faces and four wings, with what looked like human hands under their wings.
Their faces looked like the faces I had seen by the River Kebar. Each creature went straight ahead.

kavod.gloss: Glory, weightiness. The kavod of the LORD is the visible, weighty manifestation of His presence that filled the tabernacle and temple. It is a Person, not a fixture, who can rise, move, and depart when His holiness meets unrepentant idolatry.

keruv.gloss: Cherub, a class of guardian heavenly being. Ezekiel here names the four living creatures of his inaugural vision, chapter 1, as cherubim, the same order of being that guarded Eden's entrance after the fall, Genesis 3:24, and whose images overshadowed the ark's mercy seat.

galgal.gloss: Wheel, a whirling thing. Verse 13 names the wheels 'the whirling wheels', galgal, conveying restless, rapid mobility. God's throne chariot is never static or confined to one location; it can move wherever His purposes take it, even away from a defiled temple.

basic.context: Fourteen months after his inaugural vision, Ezekiel sees the same throne chariot again, but this time inside the temple itself, and he watches its terrible business: judgment coals scattered over Jerusalem, and then, in slow, deliberate stages, the glory of the LORD rising up and moving out. The building the people trusted as their guarantee of safety is being abandoned by the very presence that made it holy.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real visionary experience given to a real prophet in dated, historical circumstances, not merely a symbol invented for effect. The hardest, plainest truth is that God's presence in a sacred place is never an automatic possession; when a people persist in defiling His house, He can, and does, withdraw, even from His own temple.
basic.originalLanguages: The kavod, the glory, is a weighty, visible Person, not a fixture bolted to the building. The keruvim, cherubim, are named here as the same living creatures from Ezekiel's first vision, the same order of being that guarded Eden after the fall. Their wheels, galgal, whirl in every direction at once, showing that God's throne is never confined to a single place, not even the temple in Jerusalem.
basic.typology: The gradual departure of the glory, first from the Most Holy Place, then to the threshold, then to the east gate, anticipates its promised return in Ezekiel 43, and finds its greater fulfillment when the Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, John 1:14, and when, at the cross, the temple veil was torn, showing that the old order was giving way to a new and better dwelling of God with His people.
basic.christ: The glory that reluctantly leaves the temple in this vision is the same glory that comes to dwell permanently in Christ, who is Himself the true temple, John 2:19-21. Jesus wept over Jerusalem and foretold the temple's ruin, Matthew 23:37 to 24:2, standing in the line of Ezekiel's warning; but what Ezekiel watched depart in judgment, the Gospels show returning in mercy, and Revelation shows never departing again, for 'the dwelling place of God is with man', Revelation 21:3.
basic.god: God is holy, so much so that He will not remain among unrepented idolatry even in His own house; yet He is also patient and precise, moving in visible, unhurried stages rather than vanishing at once, giving every possible last witness to the city before the end comes.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Kings 8:10-11 records the glory first filling Solomon's temple. Ezekiel 1:4-28 is the same throne chariot vision by the Kebar. Ezekiel 43:1-5 promises the glory's return to a future temple. John 1:14 shows the Word tabernacling among us. Revelation 21:3 and 22 promise God's dwelling with man forever, with no need of a temple, for the Lamb is its temple.
basic.oneStory: This is the moment the temple's whole claim to safety collapses: the glory that made the building holy chooses to leave it. But the glory that departed in judgment is promised to return, and returns for good in Jesus Christ, God tabernacling permanently among His people, until the day there is no temple left to leave, only the unshielded presence of God with His people forever.

context.literary: Chapter 10 resumes and completes the vision begun in chapter 8, reusing the throne chariot imagery introduced in chapter 1, now explicitly naming the living creatures as cherubim and tracing, stage by stage, the glory's physical movement out of the temple.
context.historical: Delivered to the exiles in Babylon around 592 to 591 BC, some six years before Jerusalem's actual destruction, this vision assures the exiles that the coming fall of the city is neither arbitrary nor a defeat of Israel's God; He has already, in effect, left the building before it burns.
context.canonical: Answers directly to Solomon's dedication of the temple, when the glory filled the house, 1 Kings 8:10-11, and anticipates the glory's future return, Ezekiel 43, and its final, unshakable dwelling with humanity in the new creation, Revelation 21-22.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the exiles, visually and unmistakably, that the temple's coming destruction is not God's defeat but God's own righteous withdrawal from a house He can no longer inhabit because of persistent idolatry, while planting seeds of hope that this same glory can, and will, return.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical and visionary event; it is not prescribing a ritual or pattern to repeat. The enduring truth is that God's presence is never presumed upon, and that holiness and unrepented sin cannot coexist in His dwelling.

debates.question: Why does Ezekiel 10:14 call the first face a 'cherub' when Ezekiel 1:10 calls it an 'ox'?

views.label: Same creatures, different descriptive focus
views.case: Ezekiel 10:20-22 explicitly states these are the same living creatures seen at the Kebar; 'cherub' may simply be the summarizing term for the whole class of being, used once Ezekiel has identified them, rather than a separate description of the face itself.

views.label: A deliberate literary variation
views.case: Some read the change as Ezekiel's own visionary language shifting slightly between accounts, emphasizing that these visions defy over-precise, diagram-like reading and are meant to convey overwhelming holiness rather than exact anatomy.

debates.bearing: Either way, the text itself insists these are the same beings from chapter 1, now named cherubim, so the variation does not undermine the vision's central point: the glory of God is on the move, out of the temple.

study.originalLanguages: The kavod, glory, is a weighty, visible Person who fills and can leave the temple. The keruvim, cherubim, are named here as the very living creatures of Ezekiel's first vision, the same order of guardian being stationed at Eden's gate after the fall. Their wheels, galgal, whirl in every direction, full of eyes, showing that nothing in the temple's corruption escapes God's sight, and that His throne is never confined to one building.

typology.type: The glory rising from above the cherubim, pausing at the threshold, Ezekiel 10:4,18
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates its future return to a new temple, Ezekiel 43:1-5, and its final fulfillment in the Word tabernacling among us, John 1:14, and God dwelling with man forever, Revelation 21:3.

typology.type: The cherubim who guard the glory of God, Ezekiel 10:1-20
typology.fulfillment: Echo the cherubim who guarded Eden after the fall, Genesis 3:24, showing that access to God's presence, lost through sin, is fully reopened only in Christ, who tears the temple veil, Matthew 27:51.

study.christ: The glory that reluctantly departs the temple in this vision is the same glory that comes to dwell permanently in Christ, who is Himself the true temple, John 2:19-21. Jesus wept over Jerusalem and foretold the temple's ruin, Matthew 23:37 to 24:2, standing in direct continuity with Ezekiel's warning. But what Ezekiel watched depart in judgment, the Gospels show returning in mercy at the incarnation, and Revelation shows never departing again, for 'the dwelling place of God is with man', Revelation 21:3, and 'the Lamb is its temple', Revelation 21:22.
study.god: God is holy, so much so that He will not remain among unrepented idolatry even in His own house; yet He is also patient and precise, moving in visible, unhurried stages, above the cherubim, to the threshold, to the east gate, rather than vanishing at once, giving every possible witness before judgment falls.

crossReferences.note: Records the glory first filling Solomon's newly dedicated temple.
The same throne chariot vision, first seen by the River Kebar.
Promises the glory's future return to a new temple.
The Word became flesh and tabernacled among us, the glory's greater return.
God dwells with man forever; the Lamb Himself is the temple.

study.oneStory: This is the moment the temple's whole claim to safety collapses: the glory that made the building holy chooses to leave it. But the glory that departed in judgment is promised to return, and returns for good in Jesus Christ, God tabernacling permanently among His people, until the day there is no temple left to leave, only the unshielded presence of God with His people forever.

sermon.title: Does God Ever Give Up on People? (Ezek. 10-11)
sermon.why: Carson works through Ezekiel 10 and 11 together, tracing the glory's departure from the temple and showing how, even in this scene of God seemingly giving people over to their sin, hope breaks through, exactly the tension this study holds between righteous withdrawal and future promise.

quiz.q: What does the man clothed in linen do with coals taken from among the cherubim?

options.text: He scatters them over the city as an act of judgment, Ezekiel 10:2.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly commands the coals to be scattered over Jerusalem.

options.text: He offers them on the altar as incense.
options.note: The coals evoke sacrificial fire, but here they are scattered as judgment, not offered as incense.

options.text: He carries them to Ezekiel as a sign of comfort.
options.note: The coals are not brought to Ezekiel; they are scattered over the doomed city.

options.text: He extinguishes them to prevent the city's destruction.
options.note: The coals are used to bring judgment, not to prevent it.

quiz.q: In what stages does the glory of the LORD move in this chapter?

options.text: From above the cherubim in the Most Holy Place, to the threshold, and finally to the east gate.
options.note: Verses 4, 18-19 trace this exact, deliberate sequence.

options.text: It never actually leaves the temple in this chapter.
options.note: The chapter records real, visible movement out toward the east gate.

options.text: It moves only as far as the outer court and stays there permanently.
options.note: By the chapter's end the glory has reached the east gate, further than the outer court.

options.text: It vanishes instantly the moment the coals are scattered.
options.note: The departure is described as gradual and staged, not instantaneous.

quiz.q: How does Ezekiel identify the four living creatures in this chapter?

options.text: As cherubim, the same living creatures he saw in his inaugural vision by the River Kebar.
options.note: Verses 15 and 20 state plainly these are the same creatures, now named cherubim.

options.text: As ordinary temple guards dressed for battle.
options.note: These are heavenly beings, not human guards.

options.text: As idols the people had set up in the temple.
options.note: The cherubim belong to God's own throne vision; the idols are what provoked the glory's departure, not the cherubim themselves.

options.text: As angels sent for the first time to judge Jerusalem.
options.note: They are heavenly beings involved in judgment, but Ezekiel is explicit they are not new; he had already seen them by the Kebar.

quiz.q: What does the detail of eyes covering the cherubim and their wheels emphasize?

options.text: God's complete, all-seeing knowledge; nothing in the temple's corruption escapes His sight.
options.note: Verse 12 describes eyes covering their bodies and wheels, picturing total divine awareness.

options.text: That the wheels are decorated for ceremonial beauty.
options.note: The eyes are not decorative; they convey the vision's theological weight of divine sight.

options.text: That the cherubim are simply strange or frightening in appearance.
options.note: The strangeness is real, but the detail specifically serves the theme of God's unlimited sight.

options.text: That the vision has no theological significance, only artistic detail.
options.note: Every detail in Ezekiel's visions carries theological freight; this one underscores God's omniscience.

quiz.q: What is the ultimate significance of the glory's departure from the temple?

options.text: It shows God's presence is never automatic; He will not dwell where holiness is defied, though this glory is promised to return and finally dwell with His people through Christ.
options.note: The chapter's movement anticipates Ezekiel 43 and its fulfillment in John 1:14 and Revelation 21.

options.text: It is simply a literary flourish with no bearing on Israel's actual history.
options.note: The vision is tied to real, dated historical circumstances and the coming fall of Jerusalem.

options.text: It shows that God abandoned Israel forever with no hope of restoration.
options.note: The wider book of Ezekiel, including chapter 11's promise of a new heart, insists this withdrawal is not final abandonment.

options.text: It proves that the temple was never truly holy to begin with.
options.note: The temple was genuinely filled with the glory at its dedication, 1 Kings 8; its holiness is precisely what makes the later departure so grave.

questions: The glory left the temple only after the people filled it with idols. What might you be allowing into your own heart, the true temple of the Spirit, that grieves God's presence there?
God's departure was slow and visible, not sudden, as though giving every last chance to see and repent. Where might God be patiently warning you right now before a harder discipline comes?
The exiles could easily have felt abandoned when the glory departed. When you feel God distant, how does Ezekiel 11:19's promise of a new heart shape your hope even in seasons that feel like exile?
The temple's whole claim to safety collapsed when God's presence left it. What buildings, routines, or religious habits are you leaning on for security instead of a living relationship with God?
Christ is the true temple in whom the glory of God dwells permanently. How does knowing that God's presence in Him will never depart change the way you approach seasons of doubt or spiritual dryness?

translationNotes.note: The word rendered 'wheelwork' describes the mobile chariot-like structure beneath the cherubim; the BSB keeps a somewhat mechanical term to convey the strange, engineered quality of the vision rather than a smooth, simple wheel.

The wheels are given a name, 'the whirling wheels', which some translations render 'galgal' as a proper designation, underscoring that Ezekiel is recounting a term he specifically heard used for this feature of the vision.

Where Ezekiel 1:10 lists an ox as one of the four faces, this verse lists a cherub; most interpreters take this as the same vision described from a angle now that the creatures have been formally identified as cherubim, rather than a contradiction.
